title: Jazz Guitar Foundations
description: The goal of this module is to give you the minimum toolkit to start playing jazz: the right chord voicings, one scale system, one standard, and the ability to hear and play swing rhythm. By the end, you will be able to sit in on a simple jazz jam.

units.title: Basic Music Theory for Jazz
learningObjectives: Identify and construct all interval types (minor 2nd through octave)
Build major, minor, diminished, and augmented triads from any root
Build four-note chord types: maj7, dom7, min7, min7(b5), dim7
Understand the difference between chord symbols and Roman numeral analysis
Spell the diatonic seventh chords of any major key

coreConcepts.description: half steps, whole steps, the 12 intervals and their sound
major, minor, diminished, augmented -- construction and sound
the five basic types and how they're built by stacking thirds
the seven chords that come from harmonizing a major scale (I maj7, II min7, III min7, IV maj7, V dom7, VI min7, VII min7b5)
how to identify and work in all 12 major keys
interpreting jazz lead sheets and chord charts

recommendedVideos.query: music theory for jazz guitar beginners
Practical Music Theory For Jazz Guitar

exercises.description: Spell intervals from random notes (flash cards or mental drill)
Build triads and seventh chords on paper, then find them on fretboard
Harmonize the major scale in 3 keys (write out all seven diatonic chords)
Play diatonic seventh chord arpeggios up one string (any key)

assessment: Given any note, spell all interval types above it without hesitation
Given any root and chord type (maj7, min7, dom7, min7b5), spell the chord
Write out the diatonic seventh chords of C, F, Bb, G, and Eb major
Identify chord types by ear (play a chord, name the quality)

units.title: The Major Scale and Its Modes
learningObjectives: Play the major scale in at least 3 positions across the fretboard
Understand and name all 7 modes of the major scale
Know which mode corresponds to which diatonic chord
Connect scale positions to create continuous fretboard coverage
Play scales with a metronome in eighth notes at 80--100 BPM

coreConcepts.description: CAGED-based or 3-note-per-string positions
Ionian, Dorian, Phrygian, Lydian, Mixolydian, Aeolian, Locrian
Dorian = min7, Mixolydian = dom7, etc.
seeing scales as interconnected patterns, not isolated boxes
Jens's approach of practicing scales in musical ways (intervals, sequences, not just running up and down)

recommendedVideos.query: how to practice scales for jazz
The RIGHT Way To Practice Scales For Jazz
How To Learn and Practice Scales

exercises.description: Major scale in 3 positions, ascending/descending with metronome
Scale in thirds (1-3, 2-4, 3-5...) in one position
Play the Dorian mode over a min7 backing track
Play the Mixolydian mode over a dom7 backing track
Connect two adjacent positions smoothly

assessment: Play the major scale in 3 positions, any key, with metronome at 90 BPM (eighth notes)
Name the mode for each diatonic chord degree
Play Dorian from any fretboard position when given a minor 7th chord
Navigate between positions without stopping

units.title: Shell Voicings - Your First Jazz Chords
learningObjectives: Play shell voicings (root, 3rd, 7th) for maj7, min7, dom7, and min7(b5) chords
Voice these chords on two string sets: roots on string 6, and roots on string 5
Play a chord progression using only shell voicings with smooth voice leading
Understand why the 3rd and 7th define a chord's quality
Comp through a simple standard (Autumn Leaves) using shells

coreConcepts.description: root + 3rd + 7th (omitting the 5th)
the 3rd and 7th carry the chord quality; the 5th is often redundant
6th-string root voicings and 5th-string root voicings
moving the minimum distance between chords (the 7th of one chord resolves down to the 3rd of the next)
sometimes less is more in comping

exercises.description: Memorize all 4 shell voicing shapes on 6th-string root
Memorize all 4 shell voicing shapes on 5th-string root
Play II-V-I in 5 keys using shell voicings (voice lead smoothly)
Comp through Autumn Leaves changes with shells, metronome at 100 BPM

assessment: Play any shell voicing type from any root on strings 6 and 5 without hesitation
Demonstrate smooth voice leading through a II-V-I in at least 4 keys
Comp through all the changes of Autumn Leaves using only shell voicings
Explain why the 3rd and 7th are the most important notes in a chord

units.title: Your First Jazz Standard - Autumn Leaves
learningObjectives: Play the melody of Autumn Leaves by memory
Comp through the entire form with shell voicings
Understand the harmonic structure: II-V-I in major and relative minor
Play a basic solo using chord tones (arpeggios) over the changes
Perform all three roles (melody, comping, basic solo) with a backing track

coreConcepts.description: melody + chord symbols
32-bar AABA (or the specific form of Autumn Leaves)
identifying the II-V-I progressions in both major and minor
playing the melody with a jazz feel
using the 1, 3, 5, 7 of each chord as your primary note choices

exercises.description: Learn the melody by ear (or from a lead sheet), phrase by phrase
Comp through changes with shells, focusing on form awareness
Arpeggiate each chord in time (quarter notes), following the changes
Solo using only chord tones over a backing track
Play melody, then comp, then solo -- full run-through

assessment: Play the Autumn Leaves melody from memory with good phrasing
Comp through the form without losing your place
Solo for one chorus using chord tones, staying in time
Name the key centers and identify all II-V-I progressions in the tune

units.title: Swing Feel and Jazz Rhythm
learningObjectives: Play with a swing eighth-note feel (long-short subdivision)
Comp with jazz rhythmic patterns (the "Charleston" rhythm, anticipations)
Use the metronome on beats 2 and 4 (jazz standard practice)
Understand and play basic syncopation patterns
Differentiate between swing, straight, and shuffle feels

coreConcepts.description: the triplet-based subdivision that defines jazz rhythm
syncopated patterns that interact with the soloist and drummer
building an internalized sense of the jazz pulse
common comping patterns (Charleston, Bossa rhythms, quarter-note comping)
developing your ear for how great jazz players phrase rhythmically

exercises.description: Play a major scale in swing eighth notes with metronome
Practice metronome on 2 and 4 -- start by snapping on 2 and 4
Comp through Autumn Leaves with varied rhythmic patterns
Listen to a classic jazz recording and tap along to the ride cymbal
Practice the Charleston rhythm and variations on a single chord

assessment: Play a scale in swing feel that sounds natural (not mechanical triplets)
Comp through a tune with rhythmic variety, not just whole notes or quarter notes
Keep time with the metronome on 2 and 4 without losing the beat
Identify swing vs. straight feel in recordings

units.title: First Steps in Improvisation - Chord Tones
learningObjectives: Solo over a II-V-I progression using chord tone arpeggios
Connect chord tones smoothly using stepwise motion
Create simple melodies that outline the harmony
Develop awareness of where chord tones fall on the fretboard in real time
Begin hearing the chord changes while soloing (not just reading them)

coreConcepts.description: using 1-3-5-7 of each chord as your primary soloing material
basic maj7, min7, dom7 arpeggio fingerings in at least 2 positions
the 3rd and 7th as melodic anchors
filling in between chord tones with scale notes
aiming for a strong chord tone on beat 1 of each new chord

recommendedVideos.query: How To Use Arpeggios in Jazz

exercises.description: Play 7th chord arpeggios (all types) in 2 fretboard positions
Arpeggiate the II-V-I changes in time (quarter notes, then eighth notes)
Solo on Autumn Leaves using chord tones + passing tones (scale notes connecting them)
Record yourself and listen back critically

assessment: Play arpeggios for min7, dom7, and maj7 in two positions, any key
Solo over Autumn Leaves for 2 choruses, clearly outlining the chord changes with chord tones
Demonstrate the target note concept: show how you aim for a specific tone on the downbeat of a new chord
A listener should be able to hear the chord changes in your solo even without accompaniment

title: Building Your Jazz Vocabulary
description: This module expands your chord voicing palette, introduces the most important progression in jazz, and gives you your first real jazz vocabulary through licks, arpeggios, and two new standards. By the end, you can comp and solo through a blues and several standards with confidence.

units.title: Drop 2 Voicings Across All String Sets
learningObjectives: Construct Drop 2 voicings from any seventh chord type
Play Drop 2 voicings on the top 4 string sets (strings 1-2-3-4 and 2-3-4-5)
Voice lead Drop 2 voicings through II-V-I progressions
Use inversions to stay in one area of the fretboard
Apply Drop 2 voicings to comp through a standard

coreConcepts.description: taking a close-position chord and dropping the second voice from the top down an octave
root position, 1st inversion, 2nd inversion, 3rd inversion
the practical voicings on strings 1-2-3-4 and strings 2-3-4-5
smooth movement between chords by moving each voice the minimum distance
when to use each

recommendedVideos.query: Jazz Chords - An Efficient Practice Approach

exercises.description: Learn all 4 inversions of Cmaj7 Drop 2 on strings 1-2-3-4
Learn all 4 inversions for min7, dom7, min7b5 on one string set
Voice lead II-V-I in 4 keys (Dm7-G7-Cmaj7, etc.) using Drop 2
Comp through Autumn Leaves using Drop 2 voicings
Practice the same voicings on strings 2-3-4-5

assessment: Play all 4 inversions of each chord type (maj7, min7, dom7, min7b5) on one string set
Voice lead a II-V-I in any key using Drop 2, choosing inversions that minimize hand movement
Comp through a full standard using predominantly Drop 2 voicings
Explain the Drop 2 construction method

units.title: The II-V-I Progression - Backbone of Jazz
learningObjectives: Recognize II-V-I progressions in major and minor keys by ear and on paper
Solo over the II-V-I using arpeggios and scale fragments
Apply 3--5 jazz licks over the II-V-I
Play the II-V-I in all 12 keys (chords and basic soloing)
Understand the minor II-V-I (min7b5 -- dom7b9 -- min)

coreConcepts.description: the most common chord progression in jazz harmony
min7 -- dom7 -- maj7
min7(b5) -- dom7(b9) -- min(maj7) or min7
Dorian over IIm7, Mixolydian over V7, Ionian over Imaj7
treating II-V-I as one harmonic unit, not three separate chords
learning classic II-V-I licks from the jazz tradition

recommendedVideos.query: how to solo over II V I
Minor II V I Guitar Lessons
How To Solo Over Chord Changes

exercises.description: Play II-V-I chord voicings (shells + Drop 2) in all 12 keys
Arpeggiate II-V-I in 4 keys, connecting tones smoothly
Learn 2 classic II-V-I licks, transpose to 4 keys
Solo over a II-V-I backing track focusing on resolution to the I chord
Practice minor II-V-I voicings and arpeggios in 4 keys

assessment: Comp through a II-V-I in all 12 keys without stopping
Solo over a II-V-I backing track for 8 bars, clearly resolving to the I chord
Play 3 different II-V-I licks in at least 3 keys
Identify II-V-I progressions by ear in a jazz recording

units.title: Jazz Blues in F - Comping and Basic Soloing
learningObjectives: Play the jazz blues form (with the I-VI-II-V turnaround)
Comp through an F blues with appropriate voicings and rhythms
Solo over a jazz blues using the blues scale, mixolydian, and chord tones
Understand how the jazz blues differs from a basic 12-bar blues
Apply jazz vocabulary (licks) in a blues context

coreConcepts.description: the 12-bar blues with jazz chord substitutions (the IV7 in bar 5, the II-V turnarounds, the I-VI-II-V in the last 4 bars)
F7 | Bb7 | F7 | Cm7 F7 | Bb7 | Bdim7 | F7 | Am7 D7 | Gm7 | C7 | F7 D7 | Gm7 C7
when and how to use it
combining the minor pentatonic/blues scale with arpeggios and bebop vocabulary
the Freddie Green quarter-note approach vs. rhythmic comping

exercises.description: Memorize the jazz blues form in F (chord changes, 12 bars)
Comp through the F blues with shell voicings, then Drop 2
Solo using the F blues scale over the entire form
Solo using chord tones/arpeggios, switching with each chord change
Learn 2 jazz blues licks and apply them in context

assessment: Comp through an F jazz blues from memory with good rhythm and voice leading
Solo for 3 choruses of F blues, demonstrating both blues scale and chord-tone approaches
Play the head of a simple blues head (e.g., Billie's Bounce or a blues melody from Jens)
Navigate the turnaround (I-VI-II-V) confidently in your solo

units.title: Arpeggios for Improvisation
learningObjectives: Play 7th chord arpeggios (maj7, min7, dom7, min7b5) in at least 3 positions
Use arpeggios as the foundation of jazz lines (not just as exercises)
Connect arpeggios to scale patterns on the fretboard
Begin using arpeggio "superimposition" (playing a related arpeggio over a chord)
Integrate arpeggios into your soloing on standards and blues

coreConcepts.description: systematic fingerings across the fretboard (CAGED or position-based)
thinking of arpeggio notes as strong melodic targets
e.g., playing Em7 arpeggio over Cmaj7 to get the 3-5-7-9 sound
running arpeggios through chord changes in time
not just running up and down -- use different rhythms, start on different notes

recommendedVideos.query: How To Use Arpeggios in Jazz - Lessons

exercises.description: Learn arpeggio shapes for all chord types in 3 positions
Play diatonic arpeggios through a key (Imaj7, IIm7, IIIm7...)
Arpeggiate through Autumn Leaves and F blues changes in eighth notes
Create short melodic phrases that start from arpeggio tones

assessment: Play all four arpeggio types from any root in 3 positions
Arpeggiate cleanly through the changes of Autumn Leaves or the F blues at 120 BPM
Demonstrate arpeggio superimposition: play Em7 over Cmaj7 and explain the resulting intervals
Solo for 1 chorus on a standard, using arpeggios as the primary melodic material

units.title: Jazz Licks and Vocabulary Building
learningObjectives: Learn 8--10 classic jazz licks (from Jens Larsen's lessons and transcriptions)
Transpose licks to multiple keys
Understand the anatomy of a jazz lick (approach notes, chord tones, resolution)
Begin integrating licks into your improvisation naturally
Start a personal "lick library" (written or recorded)

coreConcepts.description: chromatic approach notes, bebop rhythms, chord-tone resolution
understand the harmony underneath, don't just memorize finger patterns
the key to making licks useful in any musical situation
weaving licks into your own phrases vs. just inserting them mechanically
a lifelong process

recommendedVideos.query: how to make better jazz licks
How To Make Better Jazz Licks

exercises.description: Learn 2 new licks from Jens Larsen videos (write them down or use tabs)
Transpose each lick to 3 other keys
Practice inserting licks into a solo over a backing track
Analyze each lick: which notes are chord tones? approach notes? passing tones?
Review and play all previously learned licks

assessment: Play 8 jazz licks from memory in their original key
Transpose any of those licks to at least 2 other keys on demand
Solo for 2 choruses incorporating learned licks naturally alongside your own ideas
Explain the harmonic function of the notes in at least 3 of your licks

units.title: New Standards - Solar and Blue Bossa
learningObjectives: Learn the melody, harmony, and form of Solar and Blue Bossa
Comp and solo over both tunes
Understand Solar's unique non-repeating 12-bar form and its chain of II-V progressions
Understand Blue Bossa's use of major and minor key areas
Apply all Module 1 and 2 skills on new repertoire

coreConcepts.description: a Cm minor jazz standard with a series of II-V-Is moving through different keys -- excellent for practicing II-V-I vocabulary
a bossa nova standard mixing Cm and Db major areas -- introduces the bossa nova feel and key changes
never losing your place in the form
the real test of whether you've internalized the material

exercises.description: Learn Solar melody and analyze the chord changes (identify all II-V-Is)
Comp through Solar with Drop 2 and shell voicings
Solo over Solar using arpeggios and licks
Learn Blue Bossa melody and feel the bossa nova rhythm
Comp and solo over Blue Bossa, paying attention to the key change

assessment: Play the melodies of both Solar and Blue Bossa from memory
Comp through both tunes with smooth voice leading
Solo over both tunes for 2 choruses each, clearly outlining the harmony
Identify every II-V-I in Solar and explain the key centers in Blue Bossa

title: Intermediate Jazz Guitar
description: This module bridges the gap between beginner and serious jazz player. You will add pentatonic scales to your improvisational palette, learn voice leading and passing chords, start arranging chord melodies, and develop a comping vocabulary that makes you valuable in any ensemble.

units.title: Pentatonic Scales in a Jazz Context
learningObjectives: Play major and minor pentatonic scales in all 5 positions
Use pentatonic scales over jazz changes (not just blues/rock application)
Understand which pentatonic scale to play over each chord type
Apply pentatonic "tricks": string-skipping patterns, intervallic sequences
Use pentatonic scales for a more modern, open sound in jazz soloing

coreConcepts.description: pentatonic scales as a jazz improvisation tool
e.g., G major pentatonic over Cmaj7 gives you the 5-6-7-9-3; A minor pentatonic over Dm7 gives you the 5-b7-1-2-4
choosing the pentatonic that gives you the "color tones" of each mode
Jens's approach to making pentatonics sound jazzy (wide intervals, string skipping)
combining tools for richer improvisation

exercises.description: Learn all 5 pentatonic positions for major and minor
Practice pentatonic string-skipping patterns (1-3, 2-4 intervals)
Solo over a Cmaj7 vamp using different pentatonic scale choices
Apply pentatonics to the II-V-I: one pentatonic per chord
Solo over Solar or Autumn Leaves mixing pentatonics and arpeggios

assessment: Name the best pentatonic scale choice for maj7, min7, and dom7 chords
Solo over a standard using predominantly pentatonic material, sounding jazzy (not bluesy)
Demonstrate 3 different pentatonic patterns (not just linear up/down)
Explain why pentatonics create a "modern" sound in jazz

units.title: Voice Leading and Passing Chords
learningObjectives: Voice lead through jazz progressions with maximum smoothness
Use chromatic passing chords between diatonic chords
Add diminished passing chords to common progressions
Create movement in static harmonic sections using passing chords
Apply these concepts to enhance your comping on standards

coreConcepts.description: each voice moves the smallest interval possible; common tones are held
inserting a chord a half-step above or below the target
the classic I -- #Idim -- IIm7 movement
replacing V7 with bII7
how these techniques make your comping sound professional

recommendedVideos.query: Being Creative With Chords and Progressions

exercises.description: Voice lead Drop 2 voicings through Autumn Leaves (minimize movement)
Add a chromatic passing chord before every chord change in a II-V-I
Practice the diminished passing chord between I and IIm7 in 4 keys
Reharmonize 4 bars of a standard by adding passing chords
Comp through a standard with passing chords, making them rhythmically musical

assessment: Comp through a standard with noticeably smoother voice leading than before
Add at least 3 passing chords to an 8-bar progression and make them sound natural
Demonstrate the diminished passing chord in context
Explain the voice leading principle and why it matters

units.title: Chord Melody Basics
learningObjectives: Arrange a simple melody with chords underneath (chord melody style)
Harmonize melody notes using Drop 2 voicings
Create a basic chord melody arrangement of a jazz standard
Understand when to harmonize every note vs. just the important ones
Perform a simple chord melody piece as a solo guitar arrangement

coreConcepts.description: playing the melody on top while voicing chords underneath
the highest note of each voicing is the melody note
finding Drop 2 (or other) voicings where the melody note is on the top string
not every melody note needs a chord -- mixing single notes and chords
adding bass movement for a fuller solo guitar sound

exercises.description: Harmonize a C major scale using Drop 2 voicings (melody on string 1)
Arrange the first 8 bars of Autumn Leaves as a chord melody
Work on the remaining sections of your arrangement
Practice the full chord melody arrangement with rubato and dynamics
Study a Jens Larsen chord melody arrangement and learn it

assessment: Play a complete chord melody arrangement of a simple standard (e.g., Autumn Leaves)
Demonstrate how to find voicings for a given melody note
Play your arrangement with musical phrasing and dynamics (not mechanical)
Explain the process of creating a chord melody arrangement

units.title: Walking Bass and Chords
learningObjectives: Play a walking bass line on guitar (quarter notes outlining the harmony)
Combine walking bass lines with chord voicings on the upper strings
Apply this technique to a blues and a standard
Understand bass line construction: chord tones, chromatic approach, and scale-based movement
Use this skill for solo guitar performance

coreConcepts.description: root motion, chromatic approach notes, fifth-to-root movement
alternating between bass notes and chord voicings
the classic walking bass and chords approach
developing the ability to think of two parts simultaneously
blues, standards, and duo situations where the guitarist needs to cover bass duties

recommendedVideos.query: YT lessons on Walking Bass and Chords

exercises.description: Walk a bass line (just bass notes, no chords) through an F blues
Add simple chord stabs between bass notes on the blues
Walk bass and chords through Autumn Leaves
Practice chromatic approach notes: approach each root from a half-step below
Work on a Joe Pass-style walking bass arrangement from Jens's lessons

assessment: Walk a bass line through a 12-bar blues without stopping, hitting roots on beat 1
Combine walking bass with chords through a blues for 3 choruses
Demonstrate walking bass and chords on at least one standard
Explain 3 techniques for constructing a walking bass line

units.title: Target Notes and Guide Tone Lines
learningObjectives: Construct guide tone lines (3rds and 7ths) through a set of changes
Use target notes to create strong resolution points in your solos
Connect target notes with approach patterns (scale-based and chromatic)
Hear guide tone movement as the "skeleton" of a chord progression
Apply this concept to create more melodic, intentional solos

coreConcepts.description: the 3rds and 7ths that define chord quality and create smooth voice leading
playing just the 3rds and 7ths through a progression reveals the harmonic "melody"
choosing a strong arrival note for each chord, then creating a path to reach it
approaching target notes from above and below (chromatic or diatonic)
build your solo from strong points and fill in between

exercises.description: Write out guide tone lines (3rds and 7ths) for Autumn Leaves, Solar, and All The Things You Are
Play the guide tone lines on guitar -- notice how smooth the movement is
Solo using target notes: pick the 3rd or 7th as your arrival point for each chord
Practice enclosure patterns: approach the target from above and below
Full solo practice integrating target notes with licks and arpeggios

assessment: Write and play guide tone lines for any standard from your repertoire
Solo over a standard demonstrating clear target notes on the strong beats
Use at least 2 different enclosure patterns in context
Explain how guide tones connect to voice leading in comping

units.title: Comping Rhythms, Patterns, and Standards
learningObjectives: Play 8--10 distinct comping rhythm patterns
Vary your comping rhythms to support a soloist dynamically
Learn the changes and melody for All The Things You Are and Satin Doll
Comp through these new standards musically and confidently
Develop an awareness of ensemble interaction (listening while comping)

coreConcepts.description: reacting to the soloist, not just playing time
syncopation, anticipations, Charleston variations, long tones, staccato chords
varying volume, density, and rhythm based on the musical context
a standard with many key centers -- excellent for applying II-V-I skills
a classic Ellington standard with chromatically moving II-V patterns

exercises.description: Practice 4 comping rhythm patterns on a single chord (with metronome on 2 and 4)
Learn All The Things You Are melody and changes
Comp through All The Things You Are with varied rhythms
Learn Satin Doll melody and changes
Record yourself comping along with a recording -- listen back for rhythmic variety

assessment: Comp through All The Things You Are and Satin Doll from memory
Demonstrate at least 6 distinct comping rhythm patterns
Comp behind a recording of a soloist, reacting to their phrasing
Your repertoire should now include: Autumn Leaves, Solar, Blue Bossa, F Blues, All The Things You Are, Satin Doll

title: Advanced Harmony & Improvisation
description: This module transforms your playing from "functional" to "fluent." You will learn the bebop language, harmonic tools like the altered scale and melodic minor, and the ability to navigate complex forms like Rhythm Changes. These are the sounds that define jazz guitar.

units.title: Bebop Concepts - The Language of Jazz
learningObjectives: Play the bebop dominant scale and understand its rhythmic logic
Use bebop-style phrasing: eighth-note lines with chord tones on downbeats
Learn 5--8 classic bebop patterns and licks
Apply bebop vocabulary to II-V-I progressions
Begin to hear and reproduce the "bebop sound"

coreConcepts.description: the dominant scale with an added passing tone (natural 7 added to Mixolydian) so that chord tones fall on downbeats
eighth-note lines starting on a downbeat with a chord tone -- the added note keeps everything aligned
the characteristic sound of Charlie Parker, Dizzy Gillespie, and the bebop tradition
fingerings, position playing, and picking considerations
the importance of studying Charlie Parker, Bud Powell, and early bebop masters

recommendedVideos.query: Bebop for Guitar - Getting The Jazz Sound
Learning Jazz Guitar Is About Learning Bebop

exercises.description: Play the bebop dominant scale from the root, 3rd, 5th, and 7th in 3 keys
Practice running eighth-note bebop lines over a dom7 vamp
Learn 2 Charlie Parker / bebop licks from Jens's lessons
Apply bebop lines to II-V-I progressions in 4 keys
Transcribe 4 bars of a Parker/bebop solo by ear

assessment: Play the bebop dominant scale in 4 keys, demonstrating chord tones on downbeats
Solo over a II-V-I for 8 bars in a convincing bebop style
Play 5 bebop licks from memory and transpose to 2 other keys
Explain why the added chromatic note makes the bebop scale work rhythmically

units.title: Chromatic Approach Notes and Enclosures
learningObjectives: Apply chromatic approach notes (from above and below) to any target note
Use enclosure patterns (above-below, below-above) fluently
Understand the Barry Harris approach to chromaticism
Integrate approach notes into your bebop vocabulary
Make your lines sound more "inside" the harmony by resolving chromatic notes properly

coreConcepts.description: leading into a chord tone from a half-step above or below
surrounding a target note from both sides before resolving (e.g., F#-Eb-E to enclose E)
the legendary teacher's approach to adding chromaticism (featured frequently in Jens's teaching)
more modern chromatic applications
chromatic notes must resolve to chord tones to sound intentional

exercises.description: Practice single chromatic approach (from below) to each note of a Cmaj7 arpeggio
Practice enclosures (above-below) around each chord tone of a Dm7 arpeggio
Create 4-bar phrases using approach notes over a II-V-I
Solo over a standard, consciously using approach notes on strong beats

assessment: Demonstrate 3 types of approach patterns (from below, from above, enclosure)
Solo over a II-V-I where every chord tone on a downbeat is approached chromatically
Apply approach notes musically in a full chorus on a standard
Explain the Barry Harris approach to chromaticism in your own words

units.title: The Altered Scale and Altered Dominants
learningObjectives: Play the altered scale (7th mode of melodic minor) in at least 3 keys
Understand which tensions the altered scale provides (b9, #9, b5/#11, b13)
Apply the altered scale to V7 chords resolving to I
Voice altered dominant chords (7#9, 7b9, 7#5, 7b5)
Hear and use the altered sound in context

coreConcepts.description: the 7th mode of melodic minor (spelled 1-b9-#9-3-b5-b13-b7)
on dominant 7th chords, especially when resolving to the I chord
the maximum amount of tension before resolution -- the quintessential jazz dominant sound
Drop 2 and shell voicings for 7alt, 7#9, 7b9, 7b13
Ab melodic minor = G altered -- learning to see it both ways

recommendedVideos.query: Use Melodic Minor & Altered Scale To Add Magic To Your Jazz Playing!

exercises.description: Learn the altered scale fingering from the root in 3 positions
Play the altered scale over a V7-I progression (e.g., G7alt to Cmaj7)
Learn 3 altered scale licks from Jens's lessons
Voice altered dominant chords (7#9, 7b9, etc.) in 4 keys
Solo over a II-V-I, using the altered scale on the V7

assessment: Play the altered scale in 4 keys from memory
Solo over 8 bars of II-V-I's, using the altered scale on every V7 chord
Play 3 altered dominant voicings from any root
Explain the relationship between the altered scale and melodic minor

units.title: Melodic Minor Applications
learningObjectives: Play the melodic minor scale in at least 3 positions
Apply melodic minor modes to different chord types (not just altered dominants)
Use Lydian dominant (4th mode) over dominant chords going to IV
Use Locrian natural 2 (6th mode) over min7b5 chords
Understand the full palette of melodic minor harmony

coreConcepts.description: the "jazz minor" scale (1-2-b3-4-5-6-7)
another approach to V7 (particularly V7b9) using harmonic minor from the root of the I chord
knowing when each sound is appropriate

exercises.description: Learn melodic minor scale in 3 positions
Practice Lydian dominant over a dom7 chord (e.g., C Lydian dominant over C7)
Practice Locrian natural 2 over min7b5 chords in minor II-V-I
Apply different melodic minor modes to a standard that has varied chord types
Learn 2 melodic minor licks for each application (Lydian dom, altered, min-maj7)

assessment: Play the melodic minor scale in 4 keys, naming the mode and its chord application
Solo over a progression using Lydian dominant on the appropriate chord
Demonstrate different melodic minor applications on a minor II-V-I
Explain when to use Lydian dominant vs. Mixolydian vs. altered

units.title: Rhythm Changes - Improvisation Strategies
learningObjectives: Comp through the Rhythm Changes form (AABA, 32 bars) with appropriate voicings
Solo over the A section using arpeggios, scales, and bebop vocabulary
Navigate the bridge (III-VI-II-V cycle of dominants) confidently
Apply simplification strategies for the fast-moving A section
Play the head of a Rhythm Changes tune (e.g., Oleo or Anthropology)

coreConcepts.description: based on Gershwin's "I Got Rhythm" -- the second most common form in jazz after the blues
I-VI-II-V moving fast (typically 2 chords per bar at medium-up tempos)
a cycle of dominant 7ths (III7-VI7-II7-V7)
treating 2 bars as one chord, using Bb major over the whole A section as a starting point
this is where bebop vocabulary really shines

exercises.description: Memorize the Rhythm Changes form in Bb (chords for A section and bridge)
Comp through the form with Drop 2 voicings
Solo over the A section: first using just Bb major scale, then adding arpeggios
Solo over the bridge: practice the cycle of dominants with Mixolydian and arpeggios
Learn 3 classic Rhythm Changes licks for the A section

assessment: Comp through the full Rhythm Changes form from memory
Solo over 2 choruses, maintaining the form and clearly navigating the bridge
Play 3 Rhythm Changes licks and explain how they outline the harmony
Play or hum the melody of at least one Rhythm Changes tune

units.title: Tritone Substitution and Quartal Voicings
learningObjectives: Apply tritone substitution to dominant 7th chords in comping and soloing
Voice tritone substitution chords and resolve them smoothly
Construct and play quartal voicings (built in 4ths)
Use quartal voicings as a modern alternative to Drop 2 and shell voicings
Integrate both concepts into your comping and harmonic vocabulary

coreConcepts.description: replacing a dom7 chord with the dom7 a tritone away (e.g., Db7 for G7) because they share the same guide tones (3rd and 7th swap)
tritone subs create elegant chromatic bass lines (Dm7-Db7-Cmaj7 instead of Dm7-G7-Cmaj7)
chords built by stacking perfect and augmented 4ths instead of 3rds

coreConcepts.term: The McCoy Tyner / modern jazz sound
coreConcepts.description: quartal voicings on piano translated to guitar
when and where tritone subs and quartal voicings fit in a jazz context

exercises.description: Play a II-V-I with tritone substitution (II-bII7-I) in 6 keys
Build quartal voicings from each note of a major scale on strings 1-2-3-4
Comp through a standard, replacing some V7 chords with tritone subs
Use quartal voicings to comp through a modal section (e.g., Dm7 for 8 bars)
Improvise over tritone substitution changes (solo over Dm7-Db7-Cmaj7)

assessment: Explain and demonstrate tritone substitution with correct voice leading
Comp through a standard incorporating tritone subs naturally
Play quartal voicings across the fretboard and use them in comping
Solo over a II-bII7-I progression, clearly outlining the chromatic movement

title: Advanced Techniques & Modern Jazz
description: This module takes you into the deep end. You will study Coltrane's harmonic innovations, explore "outside" playing, and use advanced structures like upper structure triads and triad pairs. This is the module that separates the serious jazz musician from the casual player.

units.title: Outside Playing and Side-Slipping
learningObjectives: Understand the concept of "outside" playing (intentionally playing notes outside the key)
Apply side-slipping: displacing a phrase by a half step, then resolving back
Use outside playing to create tension and release in solos
Control the degree of "outness" -- from subtle chromaticism to bold displacement
Listen to and analyze outside playing in recordings

coreConcepts.description: intentionally leaving the "inside" (diatonic) notes to create tension, then resolving back
playing a familiar pattern a half-step above or below, then resolving to the original key
the musical principle that makes outside playing work -- you must resolve
slight chromatic tension vs. full tonal displacement
Pat Metheny, John Scofield, Kurt Rosenwinkel

exercises.description: Take a II-V-I lick and play it a half-step up, then resolve to the correct key
Practice side-slipping over a Dm7 vamp: play "inside" 2 bars, "outside" 1 bar, resolve 1 bar
Transcribe 4 bars of outside playing from a Pat Metheny or Scofield recording
Apply side-slipping to a standard -- choose specific moments to go outside
Practice pentatonic side-slipping: play a pentatonic scale a half-step away, then return

assessment: Solo over a II-V-I demonstrating controlled outside playing with clear resolution
Explain the concept of tension and release in outside playing
Apply side-slipping to at least 2 different spots in a standard
Demonstrate the difference between outside playing and simply playing "wrong notes"

units.title: Coltrane Patterns and Giant Steps
learningObjectives: Understand "Coltrane changes" (the cycle of major thirds)
Navigate the chord progression of Giant Steps at a slow-to-medium tempo
Apply the 1-2-3-5 pattern and other Coltrane-associated melodic patterns
Understand how Coltrane changes relate to standard progressions (Coltrane substitutions)
Solo over Giant Steps changes using arpeggios and patterns

coreConcepts.description: chord progressions that move through key centers a major third apart (C -- E -- Ab -- C)
Coltrane's landmark composition, the "final exam" of jazz harmony
a melodic pattern Coltrane used extensively, applied to each key center
applying the cycle of thirds to replace standard II-V-I progressions
slow tempo, arpeggio-based navigation, then adding patterns

exercises.description: Play arpeggios through the Giant Steps changes at a slow tempo (60 BPM)
Practice the 1-2-3-5 pattern in all 12 keys
Apply the 1-2-3-5 pattern to the Giant Steps changes
Practice the 3-key-center cycle: Bb major -- G major -- Eb major arpeggios
Solo over Giant Steps backing track, gradually increasing tempo

assessment: Play through the Giant Steps changes using arpeggios at 100 BPM
Demonstrate the 1-2-3-5 pattern in 6 keys
Solo over Giant Steps for 1 chorus with clear harmonic navigation
Explain how Coltrane changes divide the octave into major thirds

units.title: Diminished Scale and Lydian Dominant Applications
learningObjectives: Play the diminished scale (half-whole and whole-half) in all positions
Apply the half-whole diminished scale to dominant 7th chords
Apply the whole-half diminished scale to diminished 7th chords
Understand Lydian dominant as an alternative dominant color
Choose between altered, Lydian dominant, and diminished based on harmonic context

coreConcepts.description: alternating half and whole steps, applied to dom7 chords (gives you b9, #9, #11, 13)
alternating whole and half steps, applied to dim7 chords
the diminished scale repeats every minor 3rd -- only 3 unique transpositions

coreConcepts.term: Lydian dominant vs. diminished vs. altered
coreConcepts.description: a comparison of dominant chord color choices
diminished sounds "bright and tense," altered sounds "dark and tense," Lydian dominant sounds "bright and floating"

exercises.description: Learn the half-whole diminished scale in 2 positions for 3 roots
Practice diminished scale patterns (in 3rds, arpeggios from each scale degree)
Apply the diminished scale to a dom7 chord in a II-V-I context
Compare: play altered, Lydian dominant, and diminished over the same V7 chord
Learn 3 diminished scale licks from Jens's lessons

assessment: Play the half-whole diminished scale from any root
Solo over a II-V-I using the diminished scale on V7
Demonstrate the Lydian dominant scale and explain when to use it
Play the same 4-bar phrase three ways: altered, diminished, Lydian dominant

units.title: Upper Structure Triads
learningObjectives: Understand upper structure triads: using simple triads to access complex harmony
Identify which triads work over each chord type to produce extensions (9, 11, 13, altered tones)
Play upper structure triad voicings on guitar
Use upper structure triads as improvisation tools (playing triads over changes)
Integrate triads into comping for a modern harmonic sound

coreConcepts.description: playing a triad from a non-root note of the chord to produce extensions
E major triad over C7 = 3-#5-7-#9 (altered sound); D major triad over Cmaj7 = 9-#11-13
a systematic mapping of useful triads
playing arpeggiated triads over changes for a modern, intervallic sound
upper structure triads as comping tools (triad over bass note)

exercises.description: Map out 3 useful triads for each chord type (maj7, min7, dom7)
Play upper structure triads over a Cmaj7 vamp: D, Em, and Am triads
Practice triadic improvisation over a II-V-I using appropriate triads
Create voicings by combining a bass note with an upper triad
Apply triadic improvisation to a standard

assessment: Name 3 useful upper structure triads for maj7, min7, and dom7 chords
Solo using triadic patterns over a standard, creating an intervallic modern sound
Play upper structure triad voicings in comping context
Explain what tensions each triad produces over a given chord

units.title: Triad Pairs for Improvisation
learningObjectives: Understand the triad pair concept: two triads that together cover a scale
Select appropriate triad pairs for different chord types
Practice triad pair permutations and patterns
Use triad pairs to create modern, angular melodic lines
Apply triad pairs to standards

coreConcepts.description: choosing two triads (from adjacent scale degrees) and alternating between them for improvisation
C and D major triads over Cmaj7 gives you 1-3-5-2-#4-6 -- all the notes of Lydian without any "scale-running" sound
they create wide intervals and avoid the stepwise motion that makes lines sound like exercises

coreConcepts.term: Common triad pairs per chord type
coreConcepts.description: a practical reference
different ways to combine and order the triad tones

exercises.description: Practice C and D triads in all inversions, alternating smoothly
Apply triad pairs over a Cmaj7 chord (C+D, C+Em, etc.)
Create melodic lines using triad pairs over a II-V-I
Practice triad pair permutations: 1-3-5 / 5-3-1 / 3-5-1, etc.
Solo on a standard using triad pairs where appropriate

assessment: Name and play appropriate triad pairs for maj7, min7, and dom7 chords
Solo for 1 chorus using triad pairs prominently
Demonstrate at least 3 permutations of a triad pair
Explain why triad pairs sound more angular than scale-based lines

units.title: Advanced Chord Melody and Motifs
learningObjectives: Create a sophisticated chord melody arrangement using advanced voicings
Use reharmonization techniques in your arrangements
Apply motivic development in improvisation
Develop a motif and transform it over a full chorus
Combine all advanced techniques into a personal musical statement

coreConcepts.description: using altered voicings, quartal sounds, inner voice movement, and reharmonization
adding passing chords, tritone subs, and chromatic harmony to enrich a melody
the art of taking a short musical idea and developing it through repetition, transposition, augmentation, diminution, inversion, and rhythmic variation
building a solo with an arc -- beginning, development, climax, resolution
treating each performance as a mini-composition

exercises.description: Create a chord melody arrangement of a standard using advanced voicings
Add reharmonization to 8 bars of your arrangement
Solo for a full chorus, focusing on motivic development (avoid random lick-stringing)
Study an advanced Jens Larsen chord melody and learn it

assessment: Perform a full advanced chord melody arrangement (solo guitar)
Solo for 2 choruses demonstrating motivic development (a listener should hear the "story")
Identify 3 reharmonization techniques used in your arrangement
Explain 4 ways to develop a motif

title: Mastery & Musicianship
prerequisites: Completion of Module 5 (or concurrent with Module 4/5)
description: This final module is not a destination but a way of working. It covers the skills that make a complete musician: transcription, practice methodology, repertoire management, and the ability to learn from the masters. These skills are relevant at every stage and should be revisited throughout your journey.

units.title: Transcription Methodology - Learning from the Masters
units.estimatedTime: Ongoing (start with 2--3 weeks of focused practice)
learningObjectives: Transcribe a jazz solo (or excerpt) by ear
Analyze a transcription for harmonic, melodic, and rhythmic content
Incorporate transcribed vocabulary into your own playing
Develop your ear through the transcription process
Build a personal transcription library over time

coreConcepts.description: it trains your ear, builds vocabulary, and connects you to the jazz tradition in a way no other practice method can
Jens's step-by-step method (slow down the recording, learn phrase by phrase, figure out the notes, write them down, analyze, memorize)
start with short phrases (2--4 bars), not entire solos
what scales, arpeggios, and patterns is the soloist using?
transposing transcribed phrases and using them in your own solos

exercises.description: Choose a short solo excerpt (4--8 bars) and slow it down to 50% speed
Learn the phrase by ear, note by note, on guitar
Write out what you hear (standard notation or tab)
Analyze: identify the chord tones, approach notes, and patterns used
Transpose the phrase to 2 other keys and practice it

assessment: Complete a full transcription of at least 1 chorus of a jazz solo (or 3 shorter excerpts)
Perform the transcription from memory along with the recording
Analyze the transcription: identify arpeggios, scales, approach notes, and motivic ideas
Play 3 phrases from your transcription in different keys, integrated into your own solo

units.title: Jazz Phrasing Refinement
units.estimatedTime: Ongoing (start with 2 weeks of focused practice)
learningObjectives: Play with dynamic variation (not everything at the same volume)
Use space and silence as part of your musical vocabulary
Develop rhythmic sophistication: delayed attacks, anticipations, behind-the-beat phrasing
Vary articulation: legato, staccato, accents, ghost notes
Make your playing "speak" -- phrasing that sounds like a human voice

coreConcepts.term: Phrasing is what separates good from great
coreConcepts.description: two players can know the same notes, but phrasing determines who sounds like a jazz musician
Miles Davis's famous principle -- the notes you don't play matter as much as the ones you do
playing soft-loud-soft, building intensity, pulling back
playing on top of, behind, or ahead of the beat for different feels
hammer-ons, pull-offs, slides, accents, and ghost notes create the "vocal" quality of great jazz phrasing
the best way to improve phrasing is to internalize great players' phrasing through listening

recommendedVideos.query: Learn Great Jazz Phrasing And Vocabulary By Studying The Masters

exercises.description: Solo for 2 choruses, leaving at least 2 beats of space between every phrase
Play a solo with exaggerated dynamics: whisper, then shout, then whisper
Practice behind-the-beat phrasing: play eighth notes slightly behind the metronome click
Transcribe 4 bars and match the original articulation exactly (slides, accents, ghost notes)
Record yourself and listen critically: does it "breathe"? Are there dynamics?

assessment: Solo for 3 choruses demonstrating space, dynamics, and rhythmic variety
A listener should notice intentional use of silence in your solo
Demonstrate 3 different articulation techniques in a single phrase
Your solo should not sound like "running scales" -- it should sound conversational

units.title: Practice Methodology - How to Practice Efficiently
units.estimatedTime: 1--2 weeks to set up; ongoing refinement
learningObjectives: Design an effective daily practice routine (45--90 minutes)
Balance technique, repertoire, ear training, and creative practice
Use a metronome and backing tracks effectively
Practice with intention: know what you're working on and why
Track progress and adjust your routine over time

coreConcepts.description: practice things in a musical context, not just as exercises
technique, vocabulary/language, repertoire, ear training, creative/free play
quality over quantity -- focused practice beats mindless noodling
your best friend and worst enemy -- use it wisely
the paradox that slow, careful practice leads to faster progress
tracking what you work on, what's improving, and what needs attention

recommendedVideos.query: Practice Strategies And Advice On Learning Jazz

exercises.description: Component
Warm-up / Technique
Vocabulary / Language
Repertoire
Ear Training
Creative / Free Play

assessment: Write out your personal practice routine (with time allocations)
Demonstrate 1 week of consistent, structured practice (using a practice log)
Explain why you've allocated time as you have and what your current priorities are
Show improvement in a specific area you've been focused on

units.title: Building and Maintaining a Repertoire
learningObjectives: Maintain a repertoire of at least 15--20 jazz standards from memory
Know each tune in terms of melody, changes, form, and key
Be able to call a tune at a jam session confidently
Organize standards by difficulty and style
Continue adding new tunes regularly

coreConcepts.description: tunes you can play right now, without a chart, in a jam session
blues, rhythm changes, Great American Songbook, bossa novas, modal tunes, minor tunes
cycling through your tunes so you don't forget them
Jens's approach to learning a tune quickly (listen, learn the melody by ear, analyze the harmony, practice in all areas)
a musician who knows tunes is a musician who gets called for gigs

exercises.description: Play through 3 tunes from your repertoire (melody + comping + solo)
Work on 1 new tune: learn the melody and analyze the changes
Practice a tune in a different key (start with tunes you know well)
Cycle through your full repertoire over the course of the week

assessment: Play 15 standards from memory: melody, chords, and 1 chorus of solo on each
Demonstrate one tune in two keys
Learn a new tune from scratch in one practice session (15--20 minutes)
Maintain your repertoire list (written or digital) with tune names, keys, and notes

units.title: Artist Studies - Joe Pass, Wes Montgomery, Jim Hall
learningObjectives: Study and describe the signature style of Joe Pass, Wes Montgomery, and Jim Hall
Transcribe at least one phrase from each artist
Incorporate elements of each artist's style into your playing
Understand what makes each artist unique in the jazz guitar tradition
Expand your study to additional artists (Grant Green, Pat Metheny, Ed Bickert, etc.)

recommendedVideos.query: Joe Pass" / "Joe Pass Lessons and Analysis

exercises.description: Listen to one album by the artist you're studying (active listening with score if possible)
Transcribe a 4--8 bar phrase from the artist
Practice the transcribed phrase in 3 keys
Imitate the artist's tone/touch/approach on a standard you know
Integrate one element of the artist's style into your own solo

assessment: Describe the signature style of Joe Pass, Wes Montgomery, and Jim Hall in your own words
Play a transcribed phrase from each artist
Demonstrate one technique borrowed from each artist (e.g., Wes-style octaves, Joe Pass walking bass, Jim Hall-style space)
Name 3 essential albums for each artist

units.title: Performance Skills - Playing in Ensembles
learningObjectives: Participate in a jazz jam session or ensemble rehearsal
Communicate with other musicians using standard jazz conventions (head-solos-head, trading fours, endings)
Comp effectively behind other soloists
Listen and react in real time to the music around you
Handle common jam session situations: being called a tune you don't know well, adjusting to other players' tempos and feel

coreConcepts.description: head in (play the melody), solos (round-robin), trading fours, head out
listening, staying out of the way, knowing when to play and when not to
supporting, not competing -- dynamics, density, rhythmic interaction
improvising for 4 or 8 bars and then handing off to another instrument
standard jazz endings (tag the last 4, ritardando, Ellington ending, etc.)
performance anxiety is normal -- preparation is the best antidote

exercises.description: Comp along with a jazz recording as if you're in the band
Practice calling tunes: pick a random standard, count it off, play the whole form
Trading fours: solo for 4 bars, rest for 4, solo for 4 (with backing track)
Practice standard jazz endings on 3 tunes
Attend a jam session or play with another musician at least once per week

assessment: Participate in a jam session or duo/trio performance
Comp behind a soloist for 3 choruses, demonstrating dynamic comping
Call a tune and lead the band through head-solos-head
Trade fours for 2 choruses without losing the form
Receive feedback from other musicians and incorporate it
